1: Theme. The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are bold as a lion — the lion-boldness of the righteous is grounded in their clear conscience before God. Christ is the lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) who advanced toward the cross without flinching — the maximum embodiment of this proverb's lion-boldness. Those united to him share in his boldness: let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace (Hebrews 4:16). The bold confidence is not self-generated but participatory — the righteous are bold as the lion because they stand in the Lion.
2: Revelation of God. When a land transgresses, it has many rulers, but with a man of understanding and knowledge, stability endures — the man of understanding who sustains stability amid national transgression. Christ is the one in whom both understanding and knowledge are hidden (Colossians 2:3), whose reign provides the stability that no political arrangement can produce. His eternal kingdom does not cycle through the coups and short reigns that national sin produces — the land where his rule is acknowledged has the stability that a thousand human counselors cannot secure.
3: Revelation of God. A poor man who oppresses the poor is like a driving rain that leaves no food — exploitation within vulnerability as destruction rather than nourishment. Christ identifies with the poor (Matthew 8:20; 2 Corinthians 8:9) and forbids the exploitation of the weak. His parable of the unmerciful servant (Matthew 18:23-35) is the NT narrative of this proverb: the servant forgiven an enormous debt then squeezes a fellow servant is the poor-man-oppressing-poor — and the judgment falls accordingly. Christ calls his people to be the rain that nourishes rather than destroys.
4: Theme. Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law strive against them — moral alignment produces social alliance. Christ's own ministry created this dynamic: those who kept the law's spirit (the poor, tax collectors, prostitutes) were drawn to him; those who praised the wicked (collaborating with Rome, exploiting the temple economy) set themselves against him. John 3:20-21: everyone who does wicked things hates the light... but whoever does what is true comes to the light — the alignment the proverb observes.
5: Revelation of God. Evil men do not understand justice, but those who seek the LORD understand it fully — justice-perception conditioned by God-seeking. John 8:43: why do you not understand what I say? It is because you cannot bear to hear my word. The inability to understand justice is not intellectual limitation but moral closure — the Pharisees cannot perceive Christ's justice because their seeking is misdirected. Those who seek the Father find that Christ himself is the one the Father seeks to reveal (John 6:37, 44), and in finding him they find the source of all justice.
6: Type. Better a poor man who walks in integrity than a rich man who is crooked in his ways — the better-than of honest poverty over wealthy corruption. Christ, though rich, became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9) — and walked in integrity throughout his earthly life. The incarnation is the maximum version of this proverb's preference: the one who possessed all things chose the poor man's path so that those impoverished by sin might become rich in him. His poverty was the integrity that purchased what no corrupt wealth could buy.
7: Theme. The one who keeps the law is a son who makes a glad father, but a companion of gluttons shames his father — the law-keeping son as the father's joy. Christ is the Son who perfectly kept the law (Matthew 5:17; Galatians 4:4) and in whom the Father found complete delight (Matthew 3:17). Those who are adopted into sonship through Christ participate in this law-keeping through the Spirit (Romans 8:4) and become sons who make the Father glad — not through their own achievement but through union with the Son who fulfilled the law in their place.
8: Theme. Whoever multiplies his wealth by interest from the poor will gather it for him who is generous to the poor — the providential redistribution of extracted wealth to the generous. The Zacchaeus episode (Luke 19:1-10) is the narrative form of this redistribution: the tax collector who had extracted from the poor encounters Christ and immediately pledges to give half his goods to the poor and restore fourfold what he had taken. The meeting with Christ triggered the redistribution Proverbs prophesies — the wealth gathered from the poor returned to the poor through the generous response that Christ's presence evokes.
9: Revelation of God. If one turns away his ear from hearing the law, even his prayer is an abomination — ritual prayer without ethical responsiveness is rejected by God. Christ develops this into the parable of the Pharisee and tax collector (Luke 18:9-14): the Pharisee's prayer is the abomination of the one who hears the law without moral transformation; the tax collector's prayer is the prayer of the one who has heard the law's verdict and responds with genuine contrition. The accepted prayer is not the lengthy one but the honest one from the person who has not turned away from what the law reveals.
10: Theme. Whoever misleads the upright into an evil way will fall into his own pit, but the blameless will have a goodly inheritance — the self-made pit. Christ's woes against those who mislead (Matthew 23:13-15: woe to you scribes and Pharisees, for you shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces) are the most direct NT expression of this verdict: those who mislead the upright fall into the pit they dug. The blameless who inherit good are those whose path was not deflected by the misleaders — whose inheritance is the kingdom secured through Christ's blameless life.
11: Theme. A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man who has understanding sees through him — wealth-generated self-opinion punctured by wisdom. The disciples were predominantly from the poor and laboring class — fishermen, tax collectors — whose lack of social position gave them the perceptive capacity to see through the wisdom-pretense of the wealthy. Christ explicitly thanks the Father for hiding things from the wise and learned and revealing them to little children (Matthew 11:25-26). The poverty of spirit (Matthew 5:3) that produces the capacity to see truly is the beatitude form of this proverb.
12: Theme. When the righteous triumph, there is great glory; but when the wicked rise, people hide themselves — the social barometer. The resurrection is the triumph of the righteous — the great glory of Easter morning: why do you seek the living among the dead? (Luke 24:5). When Christ is lifted up (John 12:32), he draws all people to himself rather than making them hide. The contrast with the wicked's rise — under which people hide — maps precisely onto the eschatological contrast between Christ's reign and the beast-empires of Revelation under which the inhabitants of the earth hide in caves (Revelation 6:15-16).
13: Direct. Whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper, but he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy — the two-movement pattern of confession and abandonment that leads to mercy. John's formulation is the NT form: if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9). The mercy promised in Proverbs is now grounded specifically in Christ's atoning work — the faithful-and-just forgiveness is possible because Christ bore the sin that was confessed. The concealing that prevents prosperity is the refusal of the cross's offer: acknowledgment of need as the condition of receiving grace.
14: Theme. Blessed is the one who fears the LORD always, but whoever hardens his heart will fall into calamity — the beatitude form for the continuously God-fearing. Hebrews develops the hardened heart warning extensively (Hebrews 3:7-15; Proverbs 4:7, citing Ps 95): today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. The hardening is a specific refusal of Christ's voice — the one speaking is greater than Moses (Hebrews 3:3), and to harden against him is to forfeit the rest that remains (Hebrews 4:9-11). The fear of the LORD that the proverb pronounces blessed is realized in the one who receives Christ's word with softened receptivity.
15: Type. Like a roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a poor people — the predator-king as the antithesis of the shepherd-king. Daniel's vision of world empires as beasts (Daniel 7:3-7) uses precisely the lion and bear imagery for kingdoms that devour their people. Christ presents himself as the good shepherd — not the predator but the one who lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11). The lion-and-bear predators of Proverbs anticipate the beast-empires of Daniel and Revelation; the shepherd-king who comes is the countertype who ends the predator's reign.
16: Revelation of God. A ruler who lacks understanding is a cruel oppressor, but he who hates unjust gain will prolong his days — understanding as the alternative to cruelty. Christ's governance is characterized by understanding in the fullest sense: he knows what is in man (John 2:25); he understands the people he leads without needing anyone to inform him. His reign prolongs not merely days but eternity — the throne upheld by faithful justice (Proverbs 29:14; Luke 1:32-33) is the ruler-who-hates-unjust-gain carried to its eschatological fulfillment in the king whose kingdom has no end.
17: Theme. If one is burdened with the blood of another, he will flee until death — the bloodguilt that pursues without relief. Christ is the cities of refuge in person: come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest (Matthew 11:28). The one burdened with blood can flee to Christ and find the relief the cities of refuge provided to the accidental killer — but with a deeper resolution: the blood-burden is not merely managed but taken away, because Christ's blood speaks a better word than the blood of Abel (Hebrews 12:24), covering rather than crying out for vengeance.
18: Theme. Whoever walks in integrity will be delivered, but he who is crooked in his ways will suddenly fall — integrity as deliverance. Christ walked in complete integrity through every temptation (Hebrews 4:15) and his walking-in-integrity was the path that led through death to resurrection — the ultimate deliverance. Those united to him are reckoned as walking in integrity (Romans 8:1: no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus) — their deliverance is his integrity credited to them. The sudden fall of the crooked is the collapse of the self-constructed duplicity that cannot sustain itself under God's scrutiny.
19: Theme. Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but he who follows worthless pursuits will have plenty of poverty — the labor-bread / empty-pursuit-poverty contrast. Christ's instruction about work in the kingdom follows the same pattern: do not work for the food that perishes, but for the food that endures to eternal life (John 6:27). The worthless pursuits are those aimed at the perishing food; the land-working of the kingdom is the labor directed toward eternal outcomes. The bread that results from proper kingdom-labor is ultimately Christ himself (John 6:35) — the bread of life that satisfies every hunger.
20: Theme. A faithful man will abound with blessings, but whoever hastens to be rich will not go unpunished — faithfulness as the route to abundance. The parable of the talents ends with exactly this formula: well done, good and faithful servant... enter into the joy of your master (Matthew 25:21) — the faithful man who abounds with blessings. The one who buries his talent to avoid risk is the one who hastens away from risk and loses everything. Christ's own faithfulness to the Father through suffering is the model of the faithful man — the blessings he receives through resurrection are the abound-with-blessings the proverb promises.
21: Revelation of God. To show partiality is not good, yet for a piece of bread a man will do wrong — the cheapness of corruption. Christ's parable of the unjust judge (Luke 18:1-8) contrasts the corrupt judge who finally acts not from justice but from inconvenience with the God who will vindicate his elect. The piece-of-bread corruption is the mundane reality Christ acknowledges — which is why he instructs his disciples to persist in prayer to the God whose justice is not for sale. The divine judge who shows no partiality (Acts 10:34; Romans 2:11) is the countertype to the proverb's corrupt partial judge.
22: Theme. A stingy man hastens after wealth and does not know that poverty will come upon him — the stingy-man's self-defeating poverty. The rich young ruler (Matthew 19:16-22) is the narrative embodiment: he hastened after wealth and kept it, and departed poor — lacking the one thing he needed. Christ's invitation (go, sell all you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven) is the antidote to the stingy man's unknowing poverty: the trading of temporal accumulation for the wealth that does not fail.
23: Theme. Whoever rebukes a man will afterward find more favor than he who flatters with his tongue — honest rebuke as a form of love. Christ's rebukes — Peter (get behind me, Satan, Matthew 16:23), the seven churches (Rev 2-3), the Pharisees (Matt 23) — are the maximum expression of this proverb's principle. The favor afterward is not always immediate but is real: Peter's rebuke led to his transformation into the rock on which the church was built. Paul's instruction that those who love the truth are those who receive rebuke (2 Timothy 4:2-3) extends the Christ-pattern to the apostolic ministry.
24: Theme. Whoever robs his father or his mother and says, 'That is no transgression,' is a companion to a man who destroys — self-declared innocence masking violation. Christ's condemnation of Corban (Mark 7:9-13) is the precise NT case: those who used a religious formula to justify withholding resources from their parents were robbing father and mother under cover of piety. Jesus identifies this as making void the word of God for the sake of tradition — the companion-to-a-destroyer verdict. The self-exonerating religious justification is exactly the no transgression the proverb condemns.
25: Direct. A greedy man stirs up strife, but whoever trusts in the LORD will be enriched — trust in the LORD as the alternative to greedy grasping. Christ's instruction on anxiety and provision in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 6:25-34) identifies trust-in-the-Father as the antidote to the anxious accumulation that produces strife. The enrichment that comes from trusting the LORD is the added things promised to those who seek the kingdom first (Matthew 6:33). Those in Christ have the model and the power of this trust — he who did not spare his own Son will also with him graciously give us all things (Romans 8:32).
26: Theme. Whoever trusts in his own mind is a fool, but he who walks in wisdom will be delivered — self-trust vs. wisdom-walking as the path of deliverance. Christ's words are the wisdom in which walking produces deliverance: everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock (Matthew 7:24). The fool who trusts his own mind is the one who built on sand — whose self-constructed wisdom collapsed. The walking in wisdom is not philosophical but relational: abiding in Christ, the one in whom all wisdom is hidden (Colossians 2:3).
27: Revelation of God. Whoever gives to the poor will not lack, and he who hides his eyes from the poor will get many a curse — the giving-guarantees-sufficiency principle. The Macedonian churches gave out of extreme poverty to the collection for the Jerusalem poor (2 Corinthians 8:1-5) — the proverb's promise enacted in apostolic history. Christ's beatitude: blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy (Matthew 5:7). The hiding-of-eyes that brings curses is the refusal of the image-of-God in the poor person — a refusal that ultimately is a refusal of the one who became poor for us (2 Corinthians 8:9).
28: Theme. When the wicked rise, people hide themselves, but when they perish, the righteous increase — the triumph-of-righteousness demographic. The resurrection and Pentecost together are the event in which the wicked who crucified Christ are judged (Acts 2:36-38) and the righteous increase dramatically — three thousand in one day. The pattern of the righteous increasing when the wicked perish is the Acts narrative at its most concentrated: the defeat of sin and death in the resurrection is precisely the moment when the number of disciples begins its multiplication (Acts 2:41, 47).
